project: OpenTabs Platform — Browser Interaction Tools for Autonomous Exploration
description: Add browser tools for pressing keyboard keys, scrolling, hovering, and handling JS dialogs. These fill the remaining interaction gaps that prevent an AI agent from fully exploring a webapp without human assistance — enabling complete self-bootstrapping of OpenTabs plugins.

userStories.title: Add browser_press_key tool for keyboard input
userStories.description: As an AI agent exploring a webapp, I want to press keyboard keys (Enter, Escape, Tab, arrow keys, etc.) so that I can submit forms, close modals, navigate menus, and trigger keyboard shortcuts to discover more API endpoints.
acceptanceCriteria: New file platform/mcp-server/src/browser-tools/press-key.ts defines the browser_press_key tool using defineBrowserTool

Tool accepts tabId (required), key (required — key name like 'Enter', 'Escape', 'Tab', 'ArrowDown', 'a', '1', etc.), and optional modifiers object with optional boolean fields: shift, ctrl (maps to metaKey on Mac), alt
Tool accepts optional selector parameter — if provided, focuses that element first before dispatching key events; if omitted, dispatches to document.activeElement
Tool dispatches to extension via 'browser.pressKey' method
Extension handler in browser-commands.ts implements handleBrowserPressKey
Handler uses chrome.scripting.executeScript (world: 'MAIN') to dispatch a full keyboard event sequence: keydown, keypress (for printable keys only), keyup
KeyboardEvent objects include correct properties: key, code, keyCode, which, bubbles: true, cancelable: true, and modifier flags (shiftKey, ctrlKey/metaKey, altKey)
For printable characters (single character key like 'a', '1', ' '), also dispatches an InputEvent('input') on the active element if it is an input/textarea/contenteditable
Returns { pressed: true, key, target: { tagName, id } } on success
Returns error if selector provided but element not found
'browser.pressKey' added to dispatch table in message-router.ts
'browser.pressKey' added to ALLOWED_METHODS in offscreen/index.ts
Tool registered in platform/mcp-server/src/browser-tools/index.ts
Tool description explains common use cases: Enter to submit forms, Escape to close modals/dialogs, Tab to move between fields, arrow keys to navigate custom menus/dropdowns, Ctrl+K or / for search
bun run build passes
bun run type-check passes
bun run lint passes
bun run knip passes
bun run test passes
bun run test:e2e passes
userStories.notes:
The key parameter uses the standard KeyboardEvent.key values: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/UI_Events/Keyboard_event_key_values. Common values: 'Enter', 'Escape', 'Tab', 'Backspace', 'Delete', 'ArrowUp', 'ArrowDown', 'ArrowLeft', 'ArrowRight', 'Home', 'End', 'PageUp', 'PageDown', ' ' (space), and any single printable character.

The code mapping (KeyboardEvent.code) can be derived from the key: 'Enter' → 'Enter', 'Escape' → 'Escape', 'Tab' → 'Tab', 'a' → 'KeyA', '1' → 'Digit1', ' ' → 'Space', 'ArrowDown' → 'ArrowDown', etc. For simplicity, set code=key for non-printable keys. For single letters, set code='Key' + key.toUpperCase(). For digits, set code='Digit' + key.

The keyCode/which values are deprecated but some libraries still check them. Common ones: Enter=13, Escape=27, Tab=9, Space=32, ArrowUp=38, ArrowDown=40, ArrowLeft=37, ArrowRight=39, Backspace=8, Delete=46. For letters, use charCodeAt(0). A helper function that maps key names to keyCode values keeps this clean.

For the ctrl modifier: on macOS, many shortcuts use metaKey (Command) instead of ctrlKey. Set both ctrlKey and metaKey to the ctrl value for maximum compatibility. This matches how most web apps handle cross-platform shortcuts.

The keypress event is deprecated but still used by some libraries. Only dispatch it for printable characters (single char or Space), not for control keys like Enter/Escape/Tab.

Files to create/edit: (1) Create platform/mcp-server/src/browser-tools/press-key.ts, (2) Add to browser-tools/index.ts, (3) Add handleBrowserPressKey to browser-commands.ts, (4) Add 'browser.pressKey' to message-router.ts dispatch table, (5) Add to ALLOWED_METHODS in offscreen/index.ts.

userStories.title: Add browser_scroll tool for page and element scrolling
userStories.description: As an AI agent exploring a webapp, I want to scroll the page or a specific scrollable element so that I can discover content below the fold, trigger lazy-loaded sections, bring elements into view before interacting with them, and take screenshots of different page regions.
acceptanceCriteria: New file platform/mcp-server/src/browser-tools/scroll.ts defines the browser_scroll tool using defineBrowserTool

Tool accepts tabId (required) and one of three scroll targets: (a) selector to scroll to an element, (b) direction ('up' | 'down' | 'left' | 'right') with optional distance in pixels (defaults to one viewport height for up/down, one viewport width for left/right), (c) position object with x and/or y pixel values to scroll to absolutely
Tool accepts optional container parameter (CSS selector) — if provided, scrolls within that scrollable container instead of the page
Tool dispatches to extension via 'browser.scroll' method
Extension handler in browser-commands.ts implements handleBrowserScroll
Handler uses chrome.scripting.executeScript (world: 'MAIN')
When selector is provided: calls element.scrollIntoView({ behavior: 'instant', block: 'center' }) and returns { scrolledTo: { tagName, text }, scrollPosition: { x, y } }
When direction is provided: calls scrollBy on the page or container and returns { scrollPosition: { x, y }, scrollSize: { width, height }, viewportSize: { width, height } }
When position is provided: calls scrollTo on the page or container and returns same as direction
Returns current scroll position and total scrollable dimensions so the AI knows how much more content exists
'browser.scroll' added to dispatch table in message-router.ts
'browser.scroll' added to ALLOWED_METHODS in offscreen/index.ts
Tool registered in platform/mcp-server/src/browser-tools/index.ts

Tool description explains use cases: scroll down to discover more content, scroll to element before taking screenshot, scroll within a chat/feed container, check scroll dimensions to understand page length
bun run build passes
bun run type-check passes
bun run lint passes
bun run knip passes
bun run test passes
bun run test:e2e passes
userStories.notes:
The tool schema should use a flexible input with optional fields. The handler determines which mode based on what fields are present:
- If `selector` is set → scroll-to-element mode
- If `direction` is set → relative scroll mode
- If `position` is set → absolute scroll mode
- If none are set → return current scroll position (read-only)

For the container parameter, resolve it with document.querySelector(container). If found, scroll within it. If not found, return error.

For scroll-to-element: use el.scrollIntoView({ behavior: 'instant', block: 'center' }). 'instant' avoids animation delay. 'center' puts the element in the middle of the viewport, which is the most useful position for screenshots.

For direction scrolling with default distances: use window.innerHeight for up/down, window.innerWidth for left/right. This scrolls approximately one page at a time.

The scroll position/dimensions response is important — it lets the AI know: (a) where it currently is, (b) how much total content exists, (c) whether there's more to scroll. This is the key to autonomous exploration of long pages.

For the container: use container.scrollTop/scrollLeft/scrollHeight/scrollWidth instead of window.scrollY/scrollX/document.body.scrollHeight/etc.

Files to create/edit: (1) Create platform/mcp-server/src/browser-tools/scroll.ts, (2) Add to browser-tools/index.ts, (3) Add handleBrowserScroll to browser-commands.ts, (4) Add 'browser.scroll' to message-router.ts dispatch table, (5) Add to ALLOWED_METHODS in offscreen/index.ts.

userStories.title: Add browser_hover_element tool for hover interactions
userStories.description: As an AI agent exploring a webapp, I want to hover over elements so that I can trigger dropdown menus, reveal tooltips, expand hover states, and discover hidden navigation that only appears on mouseover.
acceptanceCriteria: New file platform/mcp-server/src/browser-tools/hover-element.ts defines the browser_hover_element tool using defineBrowserTool
Tool accepts tabId (required) and selector (required — CSS selector of element to hover)
Tool dispatches to extension via 'browser.hoverElement' method
Extension handler in browser-commands.ts implements handleBrowserHoverElement
Handler uses chrome.scripting.executeScript (world: 'MAIN') to dispatch mouse events on the target element
Dispatches a realistic mouse event sequence: pointerenter, pointerover, mouseenter, mouseover, pointermove, mousemove — all with bubbles and correct event properties
MouseEvent and PointerEvent objects include correct clientX/clientY based on the element's bounding rect center
Returns { hovered: true, tagName, text (first 200 chars), bounds: { x, y, width, height } } on success
Returns error if element not found
'browser.hoverElement' added to dispatch table in message-router.ts
'browser.hoverElement' added to ALLOWED_METHODS in offscreen/index.ts
Tool registered in platform/mcp-server/src/browser-tools/index.ts
Tool description explains it triggers hover events to reveal dropdown menus, tooltips, and hidden UI elements — and suggests taking a screenshot after hovering to see the result
bun run build passes
bun run type-check passes
bun run lint passes
bun run knip passes
bun run test passes
bun run test:e2e passes
userStories.notes:
The key to making hover work across different UI libraries is dispatching the FULL event sequence that a real mouse hover would produce. The sequence when moving the cursor onto an element is:
1. pointerenter (doesn't bubble)
2. pointerover (bubbles)
3. mouseenter (doesn't bubble)
4. mouseover (bubbles)
5. pointermove (bubbles)
6. mousemove (bubbles)

For clientX/clientY, use el.getBoundingClientRect() and compute the center: { clientX: rect.left + rect.width/2, clientY: rect.top + rect.height/2 }.

PointerEvent constructor: new PointerEvent('pointerenter', { bubbles: false, clientX, clientY, pointerId: 1, pointerType: 'mouse' })
MouseEvent constructor: new MouseEvent('mouseenter', { bubbles: false, clientX, clientY })

For mouseover/pointerover, set bubbles: true.

Some libraries (Bootstrap, Radix UI, etc.) use the :hover CSS pseudo-class which CANNOT be triggered by JavaScript events alone. These libraries typically also listen for mouseenter/mouseover events to toggle state, so dispatching the events is sufficient for most cases. The CSS :hover state itself can only be toggled by actual mouse position — this is a known limitation.

Files to create/edit: (1) Create platform/mcp-server/src/browser-tools/hover-element.ts, (2) Add to browser-tools/index.ts, (3) Add handleBrowserHoverElement to browser-commands.ts, (4) Add 'browser.hoverElement' to message-router.ts dispatch table, (5) Add to ALLOWED_METHODS in offscreen/index.ts.

userStories.title: Add browser_handle_dialog tool for JS alert/confirm/prompt
userStories.description: As an AI agent exploring a webapp, I want to automatically handle JavaScript dialogs (alert, confirm, prompt) so that they don't block all subsequent script execution and leave the tab in an unusable state.
acceptanceCriteria: New file platform/mcp-server/src/browser-tools/handle-dialog.ts defines the browser_handle_dialog tool using defineBrowserTool
Tool accepts tabId (required), action ('accept' | 'dismiss', required), and optional promptText (string — text to enter for prompt() dialogs)
Tool dispatches to extension via 'browser.handleDialog' method
Extension handler in browser-commands.ts implements handleBrowserHandleDialog
Handler uses chrome.debugger to send CDP Page.handleJavaScriptDialog command with accept boolean and optional promptText
Uses same debugger management pattern as resource tools: if network capture is active (isCapturing(tabId)), reuses session; otherwise attaches, sends command, detaches
Enables Page domain before sending the command (idempotent if already enabled)
Returns { handled: true, action } on success
Returns a clear error if no dialog is currently open (CDP will error — catch and provide helpful message)
'browser.handleDialog' added to dispatch table in message-router.ts
'browser.handleDialog' added to ALLOWED_METHODS in offscreen/index.ts
Tool registered in platform/mcp-server/src/browser-tools/index.ts

Tool description explains that JS alert/confirm/prompt dialogs block all script execution in a tab until dismissed, and this tool allows dismissing them programmatically — use action 'accept' for confirm/alert, 'dismiss' to cancel, and promptText for prompt() dialogs
bun run build passes
bun run type-check passes
bun run lint passes
bun run knip passes
bun run test passes
bun run test:e2e passes
userStories.notes:
CDP command: Page.handleJavaScriptDialog takes { accept: boolean, promptText?: string }. Set accept=true for 'accept' action, accept=false for 'dismiss'.

The debugger management follows the same pattern used in the resource tools (PRD 2). Import isCapturing from './network-capture.js' and check it before attaching.

CDP also has a Page.javascriptDialogOpening event that fires when a dialog appears. We could set up a listener to auto-detect dialogs, but that would require the debugger to be always attached. For now, the manual tool is sufficient — the AI calls it when it encounters errors from other tools that indicate a dialog is blocking.

Common scenario: AI calls browser_execute_script or browser_click_element, gets an error or timeout because a dialog is blocking. AI then calls browser_handle_dialog to dismiss it, then retries the original action.

The error handling for 'no dialog open' is important — Page.handleJavaScriptDialog throws if no dialog is present. Catch this and return a helpful message like 'No JavaScript dialog is currently open on this tab'.

Files to create/edit: (1) Create platform/mcp-server/src/browser-tools/handle-dialog.ts, (2) Add to browser-tools/index.ts, (3) Add handleBrowserHandleDialog to browser-commands.ts, (4) Add 'browser.handleDialog' to message-router.ts dispatch table, (5) Add to ALLOWED_METHODS in offscreen/index.ts.

userStories.title: Add E2E tests for browser_press_key, browser_scroll, browser_hover_element, and browser_handle_dialog
userStories.description: As a developer, I want comprehensive E2E tests for all new interaction tools so that keyboard input, scrolling, hover events, and dialog handling are verified through the full pipeline.
acceptanceCriteria: Test server /interactive page updated with new test elements: a form with text input that logs 'form-submitted' to window.__formSubmitted on Enter keypress, a long scrollable section (2000px tall) with a #scroll-bottom marker at the bottom, a hoverable element #hover-target that sets window.__hovered=true on mouseenter, and a button #show-alert that calls alert('test-alert') on click
New test describe block 'Browser tools — keyboard input' in browser-tools.e2e.ts
Test: browser_press_key sends Enter on a form input — types text, presses Enter, verifies window.__formSubmitted is true via execute_script
Test: browser_press_key sends Escape — verifies the key event was dispatched by checking window.__lastKeydown via execute_script (set up a keydown listener in the test page)
Test: browser_press_key with selector focuses element first — presses key on #test-input, verifies activeElement changed
Test: browser_press_key returns error for non-existent selector
New test describe block 'Browser tools — scrolling' in browser-tools.e2e.ts
Test: browser_scroll with direction='down' scrolls the page — checks returned scrollPosition.y > 0
Test: browser_scroll with selector scrolls element into view — scrolls to #scroll-bottom, verifies scrollPosition changed
Test: browser_scroll returns scroll dimensions (scrollSize, viewportSize) so the AI knows total page size
New test describe block 'Browser tools — hover' in browser-tools.e2e.ts
Test: browser_hover_element dispatches mouseenter on target — hovers #hover-target, verifies window.__hovered is true via execute_script
Test: browser_hover_element returns bounds of hovered element
Test: browser_hover_element returns error for non-existent selector
New test describe block 'Browser tools — dialog handling' in browser-tools.e2e.ts
Test: browser_handle_dialog dismisses an alert — clicks #show-alert button, then calls handle_dialog with action='accept', then verifies execute_script works again (not blocked by dialog)
Test: browser_handle_dialog returns error when no dialog is open
BROWSER_TOOL_NAMES in helpers.ts updated to include all 4 new tool names
bun run build passes
bun run type-check passes
bun run lint passes
bun run knip passes
bun run test passes
bun run test:e2e passes
userStories.notes:
The /interactive page in e2e/test-server.ts needs new HTML elements for testing. Add them to the existing /interactive route handler. Key additions: 1. Form with keydown handler:
2. Long content for scroll testing:
Bottom marker
3. Hover target:
Hover me
4. Alert trigger: 5. Keydown logger: For the dialog test: the challenge is that alert() blocks the JS thread. The test needs to click #show-alert (which triggers alert), then IMMEDIATELY call browser_handle_dialog to dismiss it. Since the click is async and the dialog blocks the page, the extension's browser_click_element should return before the dialog blocks (because el.click() returns synchronously and the alert fires in the next microtask). If this is an issue, use execute_script with setTimeout to trigger the alert with a delay. Add the 4 new tool names to BROWSER_TOOL_NAMES: 'browser_press_key', 'browser_scroll', 'browser_hover_element', 'browser_handle_dialog'.
